hero.title: Your Week in Oahu
hero.subtitle: A curated journey through Hawaii's most vibrant island. Crafted for two.

bestTime.title: Best Time to Visit
bestTime.body: April–June and September–November offer ideal weather. Warm, dry, and fewer crowds. Avoid December holidays if you want affordable rates.

getting.title: Getting There from LA
getting.body: Direct flights from LAX take about 5.5 hours. Hawaiian Airlines and Alaska Airlines offer frequent nonstops. Book 6–8 weeks ahead for best fares ($300–$500 round trip).

waikiki.name: Waikiki
waikiki.desc: Convenient, lively, walkable: ideal for first-timers. Great beach access and restaurant scene.

kailua.name: Kailua
kailua.desc: Charming small-town feel on the windward side. Local boutiques, quieter beaches, authentic Oahu.

northShore.name: North Shore
northShore.desc: Surfer culture, farm-to-table food, dramatic scenery. Best in winter for big waves.

itinerary.title: 7-Day Itinerary
itinerary.subtitle: A carefully paced week balancing beach time, local food, and authentic discovery.

days.title: Arrival & Waikiki
days.morning: Arrive at HNL, collect your rental car, and check into your Waikiki hotel. Freshen up and orient yourself.
days.afternoon: Walk the length of Waikiki Beach, rent a surfboard or take a lesson from a beach boy. Swim in the calm, warm waters.
days.evening: Watch the sunset from the beach, then head to Nobu Honolulu for refined Japanese-Peruvian cuisine. Reserve well in advance.
days.tip: The municipal lot on Kapahulu Ave offers $1/hour parking near Waikiki, far cheaper than hotel valet.

days.title: East Side Beaches
days.morning: Arrive at Hanauma Bay by 7am (it sells out by 9am). Reserve tickets online in advance ($25/person). Snorkel with sea turtles and colorful reef fish.
days.afternoon: Drive north to Lanikai Beach. Consistently ranked one of America's best. Soft white sand, calm turquoise water. Grab lunch in Kailua town at Kalapawai Market.
days.evening: Explore Kailua's boutique shops, then dine casually at Over Easy or pick up poke from Tamura's Fine Wine & Liquors.
days.tip: Lanikai has no public parking lot. Park on Mokulua Drive and walk the short path through private yards (it's legal, they're used to it).

days.title: Food Day
days.morning: KCC Farmers Market (7am–11am, Saturdays only; plan accordingly). The best local produce, Maui onion chips, fresh coconut water. Then Leonard's Malasadas. Get them hot, rolled in cinnamon sugar.
days.afternoon: Explore Honolulu's Little Italy of Asia: Liliha Bakery for coco puffs, crack seed stores, and local shave ice at Matsumoto's (or the lesser-known Aoki's, same quality, shorter line).
days.evening: Dinner at The Pig & The Lady in Chinatown. Inventive Vietnamese-inspired cuisine that's become a Honolulu institution. Reservation required.
days.tip: Leonard's is cash only and always has a line. Get there right when they open. The wait is worth it but the fresh-from-fryer window closes fast.

days.title: North Shore
days.morning: Drive the H-2 north. Stop at Waimea Bay. In summer it's a calm, swimmable bay; jump off the famous rock. If visiting November–April, watch massive 20-30ft waves from shore.
days.afternoon: Haleiwa town: browse surf shops, galleries, art murals. Lunch at Giovanni's Shrimp Truck. The original, the legend. Garlic shrimp with two scoops of rice.
days.evening: Sunset from Sunset Beach. Drive back via the H-1 coastal route, stopping at Dole Plantation if it's still open.
days.tip: North Shore parking lots fill up by 9am on weekends. Arrive early or use the shuttle from Haleiwa town center.

days.title: Diamond Head & Chinatown
days.morning: Hike Diamond Head crater (1.6 miles round trip, $5/person). Go early. Open at 6am, best light and cooler temperatures. 360-degree views of Waikiki and southeast Oahu.
days.afternoon: Drive to Chinatown. Explore the art galleries along Nuuanu Avenue, the Honolulu Museum of Art (optional), and the lively Kekaulike Street market.
days.evening: Chinatown food crawl: Lucky Belly for ramen, Livestock Tavern for craft cocktails, or Fete for upscale local fare.
days.tip: The Honolulu Museum of Art is free on the first Sunday of each month. Worth timing your trip around if possible.

days.title: Windward Coast
days.morning: Drive the Pali Highway through dramatic Ko'olau Mountains. Stop at the Nu'uanu Pali Lookout for panoramic windward views. Continue to Kualoa Ranch (optional ATV or horseback tour).
days.afternoon: Lunch at Kaaawa's local spots: Shrimp Shack or Aunty Pat's. Continue along the coast to Kaneohe Bay. Visit the sandbar at low tide (kayak or tour boat required).
days.evening: Return via the H-3 (one of the most scenic highways in America, cutting through the Ko'olau range). Dinner back in Waikiki or Kaimuki.
days.tip: Kaneohe Bay sandbar tours book out quickly in summer. Reserve a few days ahead via the local operators near He'eia Kea Small Boat Harbor.

days.title: Relaxed Farewell
days.morning: One last morning on your favorite beach. No agenda. Just soak it in. Pick up final gifts at Aloha Stadium Swap Meet (Saturdays and Sundays) or Waikiki ABC Stores for macadamia nuts and Kona coffee.
days.afternoon: Drop off rental car, check bags at hotel if needed, allow extra time for HNL's security lines (can be long).
days.evening: Farewell dinner: Alan Wong's Restaurant (classic Hawaii Regional Cuisine, make reservations well ahead) or Mariposa at Neiman Marcus Ala Moana for a refined, open-air goodbye.
days.tip: HNL airport security can take 45–60 minutes during peak hours. Add a full 2.5 hours before domestic departures.

beaches.title: Beach Guide
beaches.subtitle: Oahu's best shores: ranked, described, and honestly evaluated.

list.name: Waikiki Beach
list.desc: The iconic postcard beach. Calm, protected waters ideal for beginners. Touristy but undeniably beautiful at golden hour.

list.name: Lanikai Beach
list.bestFor: Swimming, kayaking to the Mokulua Islands
list.desc: Powdery white sand and impossibly blue water. No facilities. Bring everything you need. One of the most beautiful beaches in the US.

list.name: Hanauma Bay
list.desc: A protected marine sanctuary in an ancient volcanic crater. World-class snorkeling. Reserve tickets online. They sell out fast.

list.name: Waimea Bay
list.bestFor: Jumping from the rock (summer), wave watching (winter)
list.desc: Dramatic bay that transforms completely by season. Calm and swimmable June–September; enormous surf October–April.

list.name: Sunset Beach
list.desc: The longest natural beach on Oahu, famous for its legendary winter swells. The golden hour light here is extraordinary.

list.name: Kailua Beach
list.desc: A 2-mile crescent of white sand with consistent trade winds. Popular with locals and watersport enthusiasts. Parking available.

food.title: Food Guide
food.subtitle: From truck-side garlic shrimp to refined tasting menus. Oahu's culinary range is extraordinary.

localGems.name: Tamura's Fine Wine & Liquors
localGems.whyGo: Counterintuitively, a liquor store with Oahu's best poke counter. Build your own bowl with ultra-fresh ahi.

localGems.name: Leonard's Bakery
localGems.whyGo: Since 1952. The malasadas (Portuguese doughnuts) come out fresh every few minutes. Get them plain and cinnamon-sugared.

localGems.name: Giovanni's Shrimp Truck
localGems.whyGo: The original North Shore shrimp truck. Garlic butter shrimp with rice. Everyone who's been to Oahu has eaten here.

splurge.name: Nobu Honolulu
splurge.whyGo: The Oahu outpost of Nobu's legendary global empire. Miso-glazed black cod and yellowtail jalapeño are essential orders.

splurge.name: The Pig & The Lady
splurge.whyGo: Chef Andrew Le's Chinatown institution. Pho French dip, Vietnamese carbonara, and a wine list that surprises. Book ahead.

splurge.name: Alan Wong's
splurge.whyGo: The godfather of Hawaii Regional Cuisine. Classic, refined, impeccably sourced. A true milestone dining experience.

casual.name: KCC Farmers Market
casual.whyGo: Saturday mornings only. Maui onion chips, fresh coconuts, lilikoi curd, and the best people-watching in Honolulu.

casual.name: Kalapawai Market
casual.whyGo: Kailua's beloved neighborhood deli. Excellent sandwiches, local coffee, and the energy of a real community gathering spot.

casual.name: Liliha Bakery
casual.whyGo: Open 24 hours. The coco puffs (choux filled with Chantilly cream) are life-changing. Counter seating only. That's the charm.

tips.title: Practical Tips
tips.subtitle: Everything you need for a smooth, stress-free week on the island.

items.title: Rent a Car
items.body: Essential for Oahu beyond Waikiki. Book at HNL airport from major agencies. Budget $50–80/day. Reserve in advance. Island supply is limited. GPS is reliable; paper maps still useful for North Shore back roads.

items.title: Parking Strategy
items.body: Waikiki's municipal lots are your best friend ($1–2/hour vs. $35+ hotel valet). Most North Shore beaches have free lots. Arrive by 9am on weekends. Lanikai has street parking only.

items.title: Payment
items.body: Credit cards accepted nearly everywhere. Keep $40–60 cash for trucks, markets, and parking meters. Visa/MC universally accepted; Amex varies at smaller spots.

items.title: Sun Safety
items.body: Oahu's UV index is extreme year-round. Apply SPF 50+ every 2 hours. Hawaii law requires reef-safe sunscreen (no oxybenzone or octinoxate). Look for zinc oxide-based products.

items.title: Reef-Safe Sunscreen
items.body: Hawaii banned chemical sunscreens in 2021. Bring reef-safe (mineral/zinc) from home. They're available on-island but more expensive. Raw Elements, Thinksport, and Badger are good brands.

items.title: Packing Essentials
items.body: Reef-safe sunscreen, rash guard, water shoes (for rocky entries), reusable water bottle, light rain layer (windward side), snorkel gear (or rent at Hanauma Bay), and a tote for farmers markets.

chat.title: Ask Your Guide
chat.placeholder: Ask anything about your Oahu trip...
chat.error: Something went wrong. Please try again.
chat.greeting: Hi! I'm your personal Oahu guide. Ask me anything about your trip: beaches, restaurants, logistics, or local tips.
